timeline:
13:45 - Deployment of v2.4.1 triggered via CI/CD pipeline by engineer
13:47 - CPU usage on payment-service spiked to 94%
13:49 - Error rate on /api/checkout endpoint rose to 42%
13:51 - Alerts fired: PagerDuty page sent to on-call engineer
13:53 - On-call engineer acknowledged, began investigation
14:02 - Root cause identified: null pointer exception in payment processor
14:08 - Decision made to rollback to v2.4.0
14:12 - Rollback completed successfully
14:15 - Error rate dropped back to baseline 0.2%
14:20 - Incident declared resolved, postmortem scheduled

preview: # Root Cause Analysis Report  Date: March 15, 2024   Severity: High   Duration: Brief but critical disruption in service availability observed within seconds of incident onset
rca_markdown:
# Root Cause Analysis Report

Date: March 15, 2024  
Severity: High  
Duration: Brief but critical disruption in service availability observed within seconds of incident onset    
Services Affected: payment-service, api-gateway, order-service, auth-service   

## Timeline
| Time | Event                                              |
|------|----------------------------------------------------|
2024-03-15T13:47:02Z | First NullPointerException error logged     |
2024-03-15T13:47:08Z | Connection pool exhausted                  |
2024-03-15T13:47:09Z | Circuit breaker OPEN for payment-service      |   
    
## Root Cause  
The root cause of this incident was the simultaneous failure in handling a null user ID and currency due to recent changes made within the `PaymentProcessor.java` file, which resulted in three NullPointerExceptions being triggered immediately upon deployment on March 15th at around 2:47 PM UTC. These exceptions were exacerbated by an exhausted connection pool that simultaneously reached its maximum of 20 connections and a circuit breaker trip for the payment service within seconds, leading to downstream services becoming unresponsive due to dependency failures.
    
## Contributing Factors  
- Factor 1: Removal or lack thereof null checks in `PaymentProcessor.java` file prior to deployment without implementing proper fallback mechanisms for the cart user ID and currency fields, allowing a NullPointerException when attempting calls that assumed these values were not null.   
- Factor 2: Update of GSON dependency potentially introduced changes affecting its behavior or compatibility with existing code logic resulting in unexpected system responses under certain conditions without adequate testing prior to deployment.  
    
## Impact Assessment  
- Users affected: All users attempting transactions through the payment service were impacted, leading directly to failed orders and associated services due to immediate failures cascading downstream from the primary issue at hand within a matter of seconds following incident occurrence onset.   
- Data integrity: There is potential for data corruption or loss as all user activities related to payments may not have been fully completed prior to service disruinations, especially if there were transactions in progress with incomplete payment details when the issues arose.  
- Financial/SLA impact: The immediate interruption of services would likely lead to a direct violation of Service Level Agreements (SLAs) for uptime and transaction processing speed due to failures at any point during user interactions, directly affecting revenue potentials based on the incident timeline.   
    
## Resolution Steps Taken  
1. Immediate rollback of deployment executed prior to anomalies being observed in service status as reported by all three analysis agents within seconds post-deployment timeframe for March 15th, at around 2:47 PM UTC (time and impact noted).   
    
## Action Items  
| # | Action                         | Owner      | Due Date       | Priority        |
|---|-------------------------------|------------|----------------|-----------------|
| 1 | Conduct a thorough code review, with special focus on null checks and dependencies related to the cart user ID and currency fields within `PaymentProcessor.java`.    | Developer Team   | April 20, 2024       | High           |    
| 2 | Review recent changes in dependency versions of GSON as part of codebase management practices before any future updates or rollouts are made across services to avoid unforeseen complications.        | Dev Ops Team   | Immediate         | Critical (Dependency Management)    |     
    
## Prevention Measures 
- Establish a comprehensive set of code review and automated testing practices for all critical updates, especially changes involving null checks or potential points where user input may be incomplete before system interaction.  
- Implement stricter monitoring on connection pool limits with real-time alerts when thresholds approach maximum capacity to preemptively address any such scenarios that could cause service degradation in the future.    
    
## Lessons Learned 
This incident underscores a critical need for rigorous testing and validation of changes, particularly those involving direct user interaction points like payment processing where failure can have immediate business impacts due to both reputational risks as well as potential financial losses resulting from SLA breaches. Future mitigation strategies should incorporate robust automated regression tests that simulate worst-case scenarios including invalid or incomplete data inputs along with consistent monitoring of the connection pooling system and dependency compatibility across all integrated services within our architecture to enhance overall resilience against similar incidents moving forward.
